topic: Someone discovers their teenager has a secret social media account
raw_output:
[story 1]

The phone lay on the kitchen counter, its screen illuminating the room with a harsh, unforgiving light. I had found it tucked beneath a stack of textbooks, a password I didn't know, a profile picture that was suspiciously curated. My jaw tightened until a dull ache started behind my eyes. I walked over to Liam’s room, the silence of the hallway amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. When I saw the open browser window, the carefully constructed composure I’d maintained all afternoon fractured. My breath hitched. I didn't speak immediately; I just stood there, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white, staring at the stream of carefully filtered images. "What is this?" The question came out sharp, laced with a brittle edge that I instantly regretted.

[story 2]

I felt a slow, creeping heat rising in my neck. Every instinct screamed at me to slam the device down, to sweep the evidence into the trash, but my fingers remained frozen above the screen. It was a secret life, a whole other world Liam was inhabiting behind a screen. I paced the length of the living room, the hardwood floor seeming to mock my agitation. I could hear the faint, rhythmic tapping coming from his room, a sound that felt deliberate, clandestine. When he finally emerged, looking up with an expression of practiced nonchalance, I didn't raise my voice. I just pointed at the glowing screen. "Show me." The way his eyes darted away, the sudden stiffness in his posture—it was enough. I turned sharply, the movement jerky, and walked toward the door, needing distance from the sheer unexpectedness of it all.

[story 3]

The realization hit me like a physical blow. I stared at the notifications, the carefully chosen filters, the curated veneer of an existence entirely separate from the person sitting across from me. A furious energy coiled low in my stomach, making my hands tremble slightly as I gripped the edge of the desk. I took a deep, ragged breath, trying to force my voice to remain level, but it came out tight and strained. "This is... what is this account?" I demanded, my tone dangerously low, every syllable weighted with incredulity and profound disappointment. I watched his face shift—the quick flicker of panic, the attempt to deflect—and I felt a profound sense of frustration bubble up, hot and suffocating, demanding an immediate explanation that he was clearly unwilling to give.
